name: Pong
description: The game that launched an industry. Two paddles, one ball, and an entire medium was born. Atari's 1972 coin-op proved that people would pay to play a television.
quote: Avoid missing ball for high score.

name: Space Invaders
description: Taito's 1978 shooter created the template for the shoot-'em-up genre and caused a nationwide coin shortage in Japan. The descending aliens that sped up as you thinned their ranks was pure psychological pressure.

name: Asteroids
description: Atari's 1979 vector-graphics shooter put you alone in the void, spinning and thrusting through a field of tumbling rocks. The wrap-around screen and inertia-based physics felt unlike anything before it.

name: Breakout
description: Wozniak and Jobs built the hardware; the world built an entire genre around its concept. Breakout's single-player paddle game evolved into platformers, brick-busters, and pinball descendants.

name: Galaxian
description: Namco's 1979 answer to Space Invaders added diving enemy formations and full-colour RGB graphics to the fixed-shooter formula. Enemies that broke ranks to kamikaze-dive at you changed the calculus of every shot.

name: Pac-Man
description: The most iconic character in arcade history. Namco's hungry yellow disc transcended gaming and became a cultural icon, spawning one of the highest-grossing entertainment franchises ever. Wakka wakka.

name: Donkey Kong
description: Nintendo's 1981 platform game introduced both Mario and Donkey Kong to the world. The first true platformer, it established the jump mechanic and narrative framing that defined the genre for decades.

name: Galaga
description: Namco refined the fixed-shooter to near-perfection in 1981. The tractor-beam capture mechanic — which could steal your own ship and let you fly dual fighters — was a masterstroke of risk-reward design.

name: Frogger
description: Konami's 1981 action game reduced life to its essentials: cross the road, cross the river, don't die. Its grid-based movement and time pressure created an anxiety machine that was impossible to stop playing.

name: Ms. Pac-Man
description: Midway's 1982 sequel gave the ghost-chomper a bow and improved on the original in nearly every way: random ghost AI, moving bonus items, and multiple mazes. Many argue it is the superior game.
quote: Ms. Pac-Man is on the move!

name: Q*bert
description: Gottlieb's 1982 isometric hopper put players on a pyramid of cubes, changing their colours by landing on them while avoiding enemies. Its nonsense exclamations (@!#?@!) became an iconic gaming in-joke.

name: Dig Dug
description: Namco's 1982 underground action game let players inflate enemies until they popped. The tunnelling mechanic and physics of dropping rocks on enemies gave it unexpected strategic depth.

name: Centipede
description: Atari's 1981 trackball shooter sent a segmented arthropod spiralling down the screen. The trackball controller made it one of the first arcade games with meaningful accessibility — even non-gamers could play.

name: Tron
description: Bally Midway's 1982 film tie-in was the first great licensed arcade game. Four sub-games in one cabinet — grid bugs, tanks, I/O tower, and MCP cone — made it feel like visiting a different dimension.

name: Joust
description: Williams Electronics' 1982 medieval flapper had players riding ostriches and fighting lance-mounted enemies on buzzards. Its cooperative-competitive two-player mode was ahead of its time.

name: Defender
description: Williams' 1981 horizontal scrolling shooter was brutally difficult, demanding six-button dexterity while tracking a scrolling landscape and protecting civilians from abduction. It redefined what 'hard' meant in arcade gaming.

name: Dragon's Lair
description: Cinematronics' 1983 laserdisc game looked like a Disney film and played like a reflex test. It cost twice as much per play, drew massive crowds, and proved that spectacle could sell as much as gameplay.

name: Street Fighter II
description: Capcom's 1991 fighter didn't invent the genre but perfected it, spawning a competitive scene that exists to this day. Eight distinct world warriors, frame-perfect special moves, and the first true esport.
quote: You must defeat Sheng Long to stand a chance.

name: Mortal Kombat
description: Midway's 1992 digitised fighter shocked the world with its blood and Fatalities, triggered a Senate hearing on video game violence, and directly caused the creation of the ESRB rating system.

name: NBA Jam
description: Midway's 1993 licensed basketball game weaponised exaggeration — towering dunks, on-fire streaks, big-head mode, and secret characters. 'He's heating up!' became the definitive arcade sports phrase.

name: Daytona USA
description: Sega's 1993 polygon racer arrived in linked eight-cabinet setups and sounded like a rock concert. Its earworm music and drift physics made it the defining arcade racing experience of the decade.

name: Virtua Fighter
description: Sega's 1993 fighter was the world's first polygon 3D fighting game. Its technical accomplishment overshadowed its rough edges, and it inspired every 3D fighter that followed, from Tekken to Dead or Alive.

name: Metal Slug
description: SNK's 1996 run-and-gun was a hand-drawn masterpiece — every frame of animation a feat of pixel artistry. Its absurd difficulty and POW! rescue system made co-op essential.

name: Time Crisis
description: Namco's 1995 light-gun shooter introduced the cover pedal — crouch to reload and dodge, stand to shoot. The ticking clock and branching paths gave it a tension light-gun games had never achieved before.

name: Dance Dance Revolution
description: Konami's 1998 rhythm game turned the arcade cabinet into a stage. Arrow panels on the floor, pounding music, and a crowd watching — DDR made gaming a physical performance and sparked a global rhythm-game boom.

name: The First Quarter
description: The moment the first coin dropped into the first Pong machine in Andy Capp's Tavern, Sunnyvale, 1972. The quarter that launched a thousand arcades — and an entire entertainment industry.

name: Pac-Man Fever
description: In 1982, Buckner & Garcia's novelty song 'Pac-Man Fever' hit #9 on the Billboard Hot 100. A video game had generated a top-ten pop hit — proof the arcade had become mainstream culture.
quote: I've got Pac-Man fever, it's driving me crazy.

name: The Kill Screen
description: Level 256 of Pac-Man: a buffer overflow corrupts the right half of the screen into unplayable garbage. Billy Mitchell reached it in 1999; it became the mythic ceiling of human arcade achievement.

name: The Golden Age
description: 1980–1983: Space Invaders, Pac-Man, Donkey Kong, Galaga, Frogger, and Centipede all released within three years. The arcade industry grossed more than Hollywood and the music industry combined. It will never happen again.
quote: It was the best of times.

name: The Great Crash
description: The 1983 video game market crash wiped out $3 billion in revenue and left millions of unsold E.T. cartridges buried in a New Mexico landfill. The arcades survived; the home console market nearly did not.
quote: Game Over. Insert coin to continue?
